sentences.text: My name is Alex Stern.
A-L-E-X, S-T-E-R-N.
When I was born I was called Shlomo Eli Mailech.
It's a-- a Hebrew name.
In-- in those years they gave me the name after [INAUDIBLE] great parents whoever was in line.
So I picked two, because I had two brothers.
They and only one name, to get from both sides.
No.
It's 6th October, 1924.
Today I am 71 and a half.
I was born in Sighet Marmatiei.
It was Romania at that time.
It's Elie Wiesel's town.
It was Romania at that time.
Yeah.
It was approximately 10,000 Jewish families.
[INAUDIBLE]
A big percentage.
In those areas, the Jewish were sometimes even majority of population.
Well, I-- we lived in Sighet until I was nine years.
And I went to school there.
And I went to cheder there.
It was very comfortable then, in those years.
Yes.
Well, my [SIGHS] father managed a-- a big factory where they used to make bricks.
And we had a big house there.
And my father managed the whole brick factory there.
And we were very, very comfortable.
Like close to the city, but it was in the outskirts.
Yep.
Well it was one of the big industries.
It had a chimney in the-- in the factory.
You could see it all the way to Sighet.
It was tall and it was-- a lot of people were working in that factory.
But mostly gypsies.
They used a lot of gypsies to make brick, yeah.
My father's name was Simon Stern, Yakov Simon Stern in Hebrew.
And he was born in nine-- 1896.
And he was  a religious man.
He was a very  religious man.
Yeah, absolutely.
Absolutely.
Very much Orthodox.
Yes.
Yes.
Three times a day.
And Shabbos and whatever holidays there was-- very observant.
Yes.
Well, he was in the war.
He picked up carpentry there.
And he loved it.
He used to make his own sukkah every year.
And every  year it was a different production.
He used to work on it.
He used to enjoy it.
You know, they stopped making the sukkah Yom Kippur evening.
So it was like a mitzvah starting it that time.
And he loved it.
It was one of his [? best ?] times.
One of his  hobbies.
Absolutely.
My father used to sleep in the sukkah.
He used to eat every other meal in the sukkah.
My-- my bris was in a sukkah.
So I was born in Yom Kippur.
And the bris was in the sukkah.
Something that my mother always was talking about.
Very interesting.
Cecilia.
In Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, my mother, [SIGHS] of course her parents lived in Sighet.
That's  where we were then.
She was very talented.
She-- she played the piano.
She liked to paint.
She  had a  lot of paintings that she did-- did herself.
She went to school in Vienna.
She could speaking German fluently, and a very educated person.
We were a very, very tight family.
We really were close to each other, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
We-- we were six brothers and sisters.
One passed away when she was two years old.
And my mother never stop talking about her.
So, that's the way it was with the family in those years.
My brother-- oldest brother, name was Yitzhak, he studied in the yeshiva.
He was a very good student in-- in Hebrew.
And when he was 13-years-old he was already teaching other students on, you know, on the Talmud.
And then the other-- he was very, very talented.
His memory was fantastic.
We used to learn, you know, the Talmud.
You're supposed to learn by heart.
So what my brother would look through it once, and then he knew it by heart.
I had to work another whole day.
So sometimes I got a little bit [LAUGHS] jealous or even upset.
He was  very, very smart.
The other one, his name was Jeremiah.
And Jeremiah was more of a businessman.
He was all brains.
He always figured out how to make money, and how to do things, and how to-- always, always.
And the third one  was the [NON-ENGLISH] that passed away.
The third one-- I was the third one of course.
And my-- my-- my fourth sister-- [INAUDIBLE] there was Chanele.
She was born-- died at two-years-old, there years after me And my other sister was Ella.
She lives here in Del Ray Beach.
And the little one, [INAUDIBLE] Zeisele, never came back.
Very, very pretty little girl.
We used to be so together and we-- so close and.
I can't put her out of my mind.
She was just a child.
From Auschwitz.
From Auschwitz.
She was 10-years-old.
Yep.
Everything.
Everything.
My mother was cooking and baking, and taking care of us, and giving us love.
That's the most important part of a family.
She was  very, very-- I loved my mother more than anything else.
She was the best.
She was a real Yiddish mom.
Very proud.
Very proud.
Well, we-- first of all we went to the synagogue.
We were praying.
Then we came home.
And we had our meals, our Sabbath meals and our holiday meals.
And it was sukkahs.
We went to the sukkah when it was Passover.
We made a seder.
The way it's-- it's written in the holy book.
Everything was done according to the law, yes.
In our town there was a-- a larger synagogue that we belonged to.
And that was also Orthodox, but not as religious as ours.
Ours was more like a [NON-ENGLISH].
And mostly, people, they had beard and payots and very religious.
It was a  large synagogue in the town.
Yes.
The rabbi's name was Rosen.
And he was-- he had a big yeshiva there.
They used to come to study in our town.
They even came from the United States.
I remember a few [NON-ENGLISH], the way we call them.
And they were all Americans.
And they came there for a whole season.
Then they went home and came back.
And it was a very famous-- very famous yeshiva there in our town, yes.
Because he was very dedicated to studying Bible and the Talmud and everything else.
And he was a scholar in-- in this kind of studies.
And he-- he had managed a big yeshiva.
There were years that they had about more than 700 students there.
They used to have their dormitories.
And they used to have their place where they used to eat.
They got food there.
And in the latest years they've built a new-- I would call it a [NON-ENGLISH].
They got money from the United States.
And it was all brick, the way I remembered it.
It was so beautiful.
It was the  most beautiful building in the town.
So it was a big yeshiva.
Very famous.
Well, until I was nine I went to school in Sighet.
And then when we moved away, when my father's father died, my grandfather.
And my father went to what they called in Romania, [NON-ENGLISH].
His mother lived there.
And he wanted to be together with his mother and his brothers and sisters.
So we left Sighet, and we went there.
So after-- when I was nine years old we went there.
And we went to school there, and later to the yeshiva there in [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
We have-- we had a Jewish and Christians.
And it was a public school. it was-- we had to go to school.
I mean you call it, to study, write and read in Romanian in those years.
And then we went to cheder, and we studied Hebrew after school was finished.
Yeah.
Not too many.
You see, we had payots.
And the Christians never-- we had friends more in the-- in Jewish boys.
And somehow it wasn't really-- in-- in the beginning it wasn't separation.
It was just that we felt-- felt more comfortable to Jewish boys, like.
Christians in our town mostly, they were-- they worked in the fields.
And they-- [INAUDIBLE] kind of, we didn't have much to talk with them about.
So you know, we're Jewish boys.
We were talking about religion, about whatever happened.
And we had more contact with Jewish boys.
Well, [SIGHS] first of all, we were on the Romanian until 1943.
When it became Hungary everything changed.
So it became very obvious, the anti-Semitism.
They came out with new laws.
And they came out with new regulations.
You can't  do this, you can't do that.
And then we were really isolated from the Christian community.
So we had to stick together because of that feeling anyways.
So-- There was-- it started-- there were Hungarians in our town.
And there were also Romanian.
Most of the Romanian case from Bucharest or that area where they spoke only Romanian.
And they were the teachers and-- and the public figures.
Whatever.
And all or most of them were Hungarians.
There were-- they worked in the field.
They worked-- whatever it was, it was an agriculture town.
And we had a  lot of grape fields there-- vineyards.
So that was the biggest part of our town.
Yeah, he was involved.
Yes.
And the whole family was in this kind of business.
You know, they used to wine-- kosher wine.
They sent it to Poland.
They send it all over-- all over Europe.
So it was  one of the biggest industries in our town-- town.
Yes.
Well, if I look at it in those years, we had so-- so much mind put into the studying Bible and whatever.
We didn't know much more about things then.
Whatever the Bible says.
Whatever you-- you saw in the synagogue.
And as a child, you know, you play around.
But there wasn't really particular ambitions to name like today's kids.
They have their minds made up even when they go to kindergarten already.
[LAUGHS] Not in those years.
In those years it was a  different life.
You know, we just looked forward to be able to be a good Jew, to study.
And that was the main thing.
Absolutely.
My-- my father did a lot of business with them.
And my father spoke very well German.
And a lot of those higher ranked people like from the public offices, they spoke German.
And my father was very well with them.
He used to play chess with them.
He used to bring them home.
They would play sometimes in our house.
We went to sleep and they were still playing.
And he used to enjoy it.
So he had connections with them.
Sure.
Well, in those years, of course, you only knew what you were reading in the papers.
And in town there weren't too many radios to listen to.
And even if you listened to, you didn't get too much information.
But you know, in '39 when the war broke out, they went-- they occupied Poland.
Then they went to Czechoslovakia.
And then the way it went on, we were aware of it.
But just like war.
You read in the papers what  happens daily.
But things like Holocaust or they were exterminating Jews, I mean, nobody could believe it, or would believe it.
That in the 20th century-- in [INAUDIBLE], like the Germans, you could call them civilized people would be able to do something like that.
You couldn't even imagine it, not-- not believe it.
So we always lived in that-- we never changed that mind.
So until we realized what they're doing.
Not to Palestine.
My-- my mother had brothers in Cuba.
She used to write to them every week.
I remember, because I took the mail every week to the post office.
And she was talking about it.
When things got really bad, that she wished she should could go away, wherever her brothers were.
But my father was against it, because they weren't that religious in Cuba.
And he used to tell her, what, do you want them to grow up like your brothers?
And right away he-- [LAUGHS] he talked her out of it.
So that's the way it was.
Well I did.
I liked to play soccer.
I liked to run, to do things.
When there was snow, I liked to [INAUDIBLE] go down the-- the hill, we called it, with a snow-- snowmobile.
Not-- [LAUGHS] it was called different in those years.
But [INAUDIBLE] I liked to do some sports.
And it-- I enjoyed it whenever we had a to do it.
But religion didn't give you much time for this.
So we grew up more studying the Bible and things like that.
Absolutely.
I had a big bar mitzvah.
So that was a-- I remember when we moved to [NON-ENGLISH], that was another part of Transylvania.
My grandfather came to the bar mitzvah from Sighet.
It was a big occasion because my brother was the biggest boy.
And--
Herman Fried.
In Yiddish, Herschel [NON-ENGLISH].
So my brother was very, very loved by him, because he was a good student in Talmud and everything else.
And number one and number two, he kept his father's name.
His  father was-- my grandfather's father was Itzak.
And my brother's name  was Itzak.
So he was his favorite.
He has his name.
He had his name.
So every time he wrote something he mentioned his name.
So he was very close to my oldest brother.
[SIGHS] But he came to all of our bar mitzvahs.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We-- well, the party was mostly in the shul.
They gave a kiddush.
It's not like today.
They had no-- but they gave a kiddush, and they were drinking, and they were having cake, and whatever went through the  kiddush in those years for bar mitzvahs.
Absolutely.
Well, when the Hungarians came in.
That's when we-- all our happy days were gone.
It's like a different world.
Right away they came out with new laws.
A Jew was supposed to wear a-- a star, and written Jude on it, a yellow star.
And there were-- we weren't supposed to do what we wanna do.
We had to go wherever we were told to go.
It was-- it was when it started.
So.
It was 1943.
The end of '43, beginning of '44.
Well, they-- you see, you'd be surprised.
In those little towns, a man used to go with a drum and hit the drum in the middle of the town.
And the people used to stay around him.
And he used to give out the new laws.
And then when it started, the new law that Jew are not allowed to buy this or sell this, or-- or do this.
And every day this guy used to go with the drum and give out new laws.
Usually they used to be tax laws or whatever the town was supposed to do.
There was no town newspaper.
But since the Hungarian came in all the laws were against Jews.
So they told us always you don't do  this and you don't do that.
You're not supposed to travel without a  permit.
And it was-- it started to feel like anti-Semitism.
It stared to feel that they were doing things to hurt us, and take away our liberty, and to take away our way to communicate, or our way to-- to be able to stick together.
Whatever they intended to do with it.
I-- [SIGHS] I was 20 year-- 19 years old.
In 1944 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where the real changes began, yeah.
Yeah, we stayed in our home until they didn't take us out from there.
Yes, we  were in our home.
Terrible.
Terrible.
First of all, [SIGHS] we-- we lost all our freedoms.
We lost our way of life.
We lost most of our income.
We lost most of our being able to study, or to come and go.
It was terrible.
It was just like-- like isolated.
Yeah, we did go to school.
We did go to school.
But we had to sit separate.
And-- we let us-- they let us know that we were Jews, beside of wearing the star of David that we did.
And it was getting very tough.
It was getting bad.
And mostly the Hungarians.
They were-- kind of taking over the whole management of the city-- I mean of the town.
Whatever it was.
And they-- they were tough.
Not really.
Not really.
He was-- He couldn't go out from town easy.
It was somehow such an isolated life, that nobody even was thinking about business, or doing things like that.
We were thinking about how to go along with all of those restrictions, and all those terrible things they came up with beside.
All the kosher ritual laws were forbidden.
And in latest months, they were-- they weren't even allowed to go to shul, or to-- to prayers.
So it was losing the whole identity for our Jewish lives.
So we became completely isolated, completely like-- like we didn't belong there.
Well, a livelihood in those days was being able to-- to eat.
I mean, there was nothing else that you were thinking about.
I remember when in summer time, I went to work on a-- you know, the machine that separates the-- the [SIGHS] grains from the-- to make bread.
To make flour.
So we had to work there in order to be able to get bread.
